epigraph.text:
Two are better than one, because they have a good 
return for their labor.
epigraph.quote: The whole is greater than the sum of its parts.

content.text: {The whole is greater than the sum of its parts.}{Aristotle}

When a polynomial has four terms and no common factor across all terms, 
we can often factor by grouping: pairing terms strategically and 
factoring each pair separately.

content.title: Factoring by Grouping
content.content:
1. Group the terms into pairs (usually the first two and last two).

2. Factor out the GCF from each pair.

3. If a common binomial factor appears, factor it out.

4. If not, try regrouping differently.

Always verify by multiplying! If the factors don't multiply back to 
the original, check your grouping and signs.

content.text: Sometimes the terms need to be rearranged before grouping works.

content.title: When rearrangement is needed
content.solution:
The given order doesn't work. Rearrange:
$$
 3a + 6 - ab - 2b &= (3a + 6) + (-ab - 2b) \\
 &= 3(a + 2) - b(a + 2) \\
 &= (a + 2)(3 - b)
$$

content.text:
Not every four-term polynomial factors by grouping. If no arrangement 
produces a common binomial factor, the polynomial may be prime 
(unfactorable over integers) or may require a different technique.

marginNotes.text:
For where two or three gather in my 
name, there am I with them.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the queen of the 
sciences.
